title: G-functions and Holonomy Bounds
speaker: Frank Calegari
abstract:
A G-function, in the sense of Siegel, is a power series with rational coefficients that on the one hand has nice arithmetic properties (the LCM of the denominators of the first \(n\) coefficients grows at most exponentially) and on the other hand has nice analytic properties (it is the solution of a linear ODE with coefficients in \(ℚ(x)\)). These functions are expected to have rich connections with geometry.
In recent years, Dimitrov, Tang, and the speaker have been exploring precisely the tension between these analytic and arithmetic properties. In certain prescribed contexts, we are able to prove a ``holonomy bound'' showing that power series with certain properties must be G-functions of small order.
The goal of this talk is to explore the limits of our results, to raise a number of basic open questions about G-functions, and also, perhaps, to discuss some unexpected applications of our results, for example to the effective \(S\)-unit equation or to a new proof of the transcendence of \(π\).

proof_stages.label: Geometric period motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with geometric power series, especially a Legendre elliptic-curve period, to exhibit the paired phenomena of controlled rational denominators and analytic continuation through a differential equation.

proof_stages.label: Classical G-function conditions and denominator philosophy
proof_stages.summary: Siegel G-functions are presented as power series satisfying a linear differential equation and an exponential lcm-denominator bound, with discussion of why geometry suggests even more structured denominator forms.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic-continuation setup for the joint work
proof_stages.summary: For fixed denominator data and a chosen analytic map from the disk, the speaker defines a Q(x)-vector space of power series whose pullbacks have the prescribed holomorphic continuation, noting that finite dimensionality forces a differential equation.

proof_stages.label: Main holonomy-bound theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that if the derivative of the chosen disk map at the origin is larger than the exponential of the denominator-growth invariant, then the associated space is finite dimensional with an explicit dimension bound.

proof_stages.label: Capacity and punctured-curve corollaries
proof_stages.summary: Specializing the theorem to univalent maps gives capacity formulas and recovers Borel-Polya, while uniformizing punctured projective lines yields finite-dimensionality statements for continuations around zero, one, and infinity and solves the simplest logarithmic denominator case.

proof_stages.label: Asymptotic sharpness templates
proof_stages.summary: The speaker compares upper bounds with lower bounds from powers of logarithms and finite-monodromy special functions, optimizing parameters to show that the theorem is asymptotically close to sharp up to a constant.

proof_stages.label: Partial overconvergence and transcendence mechanism
proof_stages.summary: Adapting the method to functions that overconverge along only one chosen path gives lower-order entire pullbacks, so powers of such functions force dimension contradictions unless certain constants are algebraic or transcendental as claimed.

proof_stages.label: Effectivity and broader arithmetic outlook
proof_stages.summary: The final part stresses that the resulting rational-approximation bounds are effective, cites applications such as effective S-unit equations, and discusses in questions how robust the method is and how it might extend to symmetric-power or higher-dimensional settings.

examples.name: Legendre family period
examples.note: The period of the Legendre family of elliptic curves gives a hypergeometric series with controlled denominators and a Picard-Fuchs differential equation, motivating the arithmetic/analytic split in the definition of G-functions.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes the Legendre period integral, evaluates it as a hypergeometric power series, gives its differential equation, and analyzes its continuation and denominators

examples.name: Logarithm and powers of logarithm
examples.note: The series -log(1-t)=sum t^n/n first illustrates the lcm-denominator condition and later its powers produce the denominator templates and lower bounds for the P^1 minus two points analysis.
examples.engagement_reason: the logarithm example is expanded, its denominator LCM is analyzed, and later powers of the logarithm are used with convolution/denominator estimates and theorem bounds

examples.name: Borel-Polya integer-coefficient case
examples.note: Specializing the theorem to integer Taylor coefficients and a univalent continuation domain of capacity greater than one recovers the classical conclusion that the function is rational.
examples.engagement_reason: the integer-coefficient Borel-Polya case is obtained by specializing the theorem, simplifying the invariants, and deriving rationality from dimension one

passes.reason: The integral-coefficient specialization has trivial denominator data and realizes the sharp capacity-one Borel–Pólya threshold.

examples.name: Prime-number-theorem sharpness barrier
examples.note: The observation that pushing the asymptotic constant down to one would yield an elementary proof of the prime number theorem illustrates the arithmetic difficulty in optimizing the bound.
examples.engagement_reason: the prime number theorem is invoked as a known fact and as a sharpness barrier, but no proof or concrete work is carried out on it
